title: J&K CM Announces Hydropower Capacity Tripling to 11,000 MW by 2035
detail: Jammu & Kashmir Chief Minister Omar Abdullah announced in the J&K Legislative Assembly that the Union Territory aims to triple its installed hydropower capacity from approximately 3,540 MW to around 11,000 MW by 2035. The ambitious target was directly tied to India's suspension of the Indus Waters Treaty in April 2025 following the Pahalgam attack and Operation Sindoor: with the western rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) now available for unrestricted development, J&K can unlock the full hydropower potential previously restricted under the treaty. The announcement coincided with the NHPC-JKSPDCL agreement to develop two new projects — Uri-I Stage-II (240 MW) and Dulhasti Stage-II (260 MW) on a BOOT basis — signed the previous day.

title: NHPC and J&K Sign 500 MW Hydropower Expansion Deal — IWT Dividend
detail: India-backed NHPC and the J&K State Power Development Corporation (JKSPDCL) signed an implementation agreement in Jammu for two new hydroelectric projects: Uri-I Stage-II (240 MW) on the Jhelum River and Dulhasti Stage-II (260 MW) on the Chenab River. Both projects will be built on a Build-Own-Operate-Transfer (BOOT) basis for 40 years, with electricity to be supplied to J&K at preferential rates. The deal was explicitly linked to J&K's hydropower development push enabled by India's suspension of the Indus Waters Treaty. Under the old IWT framework, India faced severe restrictions on storage and run-of-river projects on western rivers flowing into Pakistan. Pakistani analysts described the accelerated dam construction as further weaponization of the Indus basin following Operation Sindoor.
